essays.title: Circle of Competence
essays.body:
The first rule of competition is that you are more likely to win if you play where you have an advantage. Doing so requires a firm understanding of what you know and don't know. Your circle of competence is your sphere of expertise, the area where your knowledge and skills are concentrated. It's the domain where you have a deep understanding, where your judgements are reliable, and your decisions are sound.

Understanding where you have an edge in competence and where you do not helps you spot opportunities, avoid problems, and learn.

The size of that circle is not as important as knowing when you are approaching its perimeter.

As you cross the perimeter, not only does your advantage vanish, it goes negative. You find yourself playing in an area where others have the advantage.

The Lifer and the Stranger
- Imagine a small town. There are two characters: the Lifer, who has lived there his entire life, and the Stranger, who has just arrived.
- Bit by bit, the Lifer has built up his knowledge over a long period of both observation and participation in the towns affairs and its people.
- In the Stranger's mind, he's convinced he knows pretty much everything a Lifer would know; with his keen eye, he's sized up the town in no time.
- But when it comes to this town, the Lifer could stump the Stranger in no time, not the other way around.

We shall be unable to turn natural advantage to account unless we make use of local guides. - Sun Tzu

There are approximately two hundred bodies on Mount Everest (not including those which have been removed). None of those people thought Everest would take their life.

The ascent to the summit takes you past the bodies of people who once shared your dreams.

Sherpa Tenzing Norgay led the first team to summit Everest, but only after twenty years of climbing, trekking, and previously failing to summit.

I never allow myself to have an opinion on anything that I don't know the other side's argument better than they do. - Charlie Munger

When we have a circle of competence, we can seamlessly adapt our language to a different level, zooming in our out. We also know what is knowable.

Within our circle of competence, we can anticipate and respond to counterarguments, because we understand them better than the person making them.

A little learning is a dangerous thing;
Drink deep, or taste not the Pierian spring:
There shallow draughts intoxicate the brain,
And drinking largely sobers us again.

One of the essential requirements of a circle of competence is that you can never take it for granted. The terrain is always shifting.

Learning comes when experience is paired with reflection. That experience can be your own, or that of others—as learning everything on your own is costly and slow.

You must honestly monitor your track record in areas you have, or wish to have, a circle of competence—we often don't keep honest records, because we don't want to know what we are good at and bad at. Ego is a powerful enemy when it comes to understanding reality.

Monitoring your records allows you to see patterns you did not notice before. This is painful for the ego, which is why it also helps build a true circle of competence. Another option is to hire a coach.

It is difficult to maintain a circle of competence without an outside perspective. We usually have too many biases to rely solely on our own observations.

We must develop a repertoire of techniques for managing when we're outside of our sphere, which happens all the time.

When outside of your circle of competence:
- Learn the basics of the realm you're operating in, while still acknowledging you're a Stranger, not a Lifer.
- Keep in mind that basic information is easy to obtain and tends to seduce the acquirer into possessing unwarranted confidence.
- Talk to someone whose circle of competence in the area is strong. Ask them what variables matter in the situation and why. You'll learn not only what they would do but why they would do it.

When Queen Elizabeth I of England ascended to the throne in a tumultuous era, she kept her council small so that real discussions could happen. She wanted a variety of competent opinions that could be challenged and debated.

essays.title: Models of Management
essays.body:
As more and more people learn what model you are using to manage them, they may decide not to respond to your incentives.

As your competitors gain knowledge of the model, they respond in kind by adopting the model themselves, thus flattening the playing field.

The model may have been useful mostly in one setting, and not in another.

Human beings are not simple automatons: a more complete management model would hone in on other motivations they might have besides, for example, financial ones.

essays.title: Words
essays.body: Parochialism - A limited or narrow outlook, especially focused on a local area

essays.title: Original Quotes
essays.body: Understanding without adaptation is like locking yourself into an eternal prison of repeating failure. But as soon as you have adapted, you free yourself.

essays.title: Quotes
essays.body:
Most geniuses—especially those who lead others—prosper not by deconstructing intricate complexities but by exploiting unrecognized simplicities. - Andy Benoit

A lot of people start out with 400-horsepower motors but only get a hundred horsepower of output. It's way better to have a 200-horsepower motor and get it all into output. - Warren Buffett

We shall be unable to turn natural advantage to account unless we make use of local guides. - Sun Tzu

I never allow myself to have an opinion on anything that I don't know the other side's argument better than they do. - Charlie Munger

Wisdom more often begets confidence than knowledge. - Charles Darwin

essays.title: Mental Flaws
essays.body:
The first flaw is the failure of perspective. It is difficult to accurately observe a system that we are inside of.

The second flaw is ego—the part of us that is afraid, proud, and always in competition. Many of us have too much invested in our opinions to allow feedback to update them. We perceive updating our opinions to be painful, like admitting defeat.

The third flaw is distance. When we see or feel the consequences of our decisions immediately, it is easier to accept this feedback and update our thinking. When the consequences are far removed, delayed, or unseen, we may not ever even observe the feedback in order to update our thinking.

essays.title: Maps & Models
essays.body:
The map is not the territory. You need to touch the territory itself to understand it.

All maps are wrong, but some are useful—provided they are not too wrong.

Maps can describe a territory in a useful way, if there is a specific purpose in mind.

Problems are bound to arise when we mistake knowledge of the map with knowledge of the territory.

The faster the rate of change in a territory, the harder it will be for a map to keep up-to-date.

Territories evolve, and so too do the tools that can be used to navigate them.

We must acknowledge the limit of what maps are able to explain, and carefully explore that which lies beyond them.

Good maps are built through regular feedback loops provided by territory explorers.

Maps tend to only show one or a few layers of often multi-layered territories.

World maps can lead us to equate nations as uniform societies, implying shared identities within arbitrary border lines. This is misleading.

Maps reflect the cartographers intent, as well as their bias, knowledge, and limitations.

Maps can be used to reshape territories.

Updating our maps can be a difficult process of reconciling what we wish were true with what is actually true.

When not making your own map, select cartographers who are rigorous, transparent, and open to feedback and revision.
